topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to bleed across my vision. I stared at the 'D-'. It wasn't just a mark; it was a verdict, a complete dismantling of the late nights and the caffeine-fueled marathon sessions I’d poured into this assignment. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic pen slipping from my grasp and clattering onto the desk. A tight knot tightened in my chest, making each breath shallow and catching in my throat. *This can't be right.* I reread the feedback, the critiques sharp and unforgiving. My mind felt like a tangled ball of yarn, every thread pulling in a direction that led only to failure. I pushed my chair back with a harsh scrape against the linoleum floor, the sudden noise amplifying the hollow thud in my stomach.

[story 2]

I kept smoothing the crumpled paper, running my thumb over the smudges of the professor’s score. Every time I looked up, the fluorescent lights of the library seemed too bright, the silence too heavy. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the ache in my molars. I started pacing the narrow aisle between the study carrels, my footsteps quick and uneven. "No," I muttered to myself, the sound barely audible over the hum of the ventilation system. "I understood the material. I studied." A wave of hot flush crept up my neck. I leaned against a bookshelf, closing my eyes, trying to force the frantic churning in my gut to settle, but the disappointment tasted metallic and bitter on my tongue.

[story 3]

The email notification flashed on my screen, and I didn't move for a full minute. My fingers hovered over the 'reply' button, paralyzed by a strange inertia. When I finally typed, the words came out clipped and defensive, a thin veneer over something brittle. I reread the rejection, focusing on the single, damning sentence. A sudden, sharp intake of air escaped me, a quick, involuntary gasp. I stood up abruptly, knocking my water bottle slightly askew. The room swam for a second, and I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying feeling of being completely adrift.
